ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried beef with Thai herbs; closest parent 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002725) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for Thai herb stir-fried beef; 'Beef, cooked' (A0CKP) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai stir-fried beef preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried beef with Thai herbs is approximately 250 g, typically served over or alongside steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables and sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Thai herb components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried beef with Thai herbs exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef stir-fry with sauce (NDB 35194) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Thai herb-based dishes. Macros reflect a typical Thai restaurant preparation with lean beef, fish sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, chilli, and aromatic herbs stir-fried in vegetable oil. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — beef and mixed meat-vegetable stir-fry dishes; analogous estimate based on stir-fried beef with sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried beef with Thai herbs. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous stir-fried beef and mixed vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with a modest carbohydrate content from sauce and herbs, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (45 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.85, rounded to 6.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 45 and only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large quantities of steamed rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (2.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts and request reduced-sauce preparation to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Beef with Thai Herbs (เนื้อผัดสมุนไพรไทย, Nuea Phat Samunphrai Thai) is a classic Thai stir-fry dish featuring thinly sliced beef cooked at high heat in a wok with aromatic Thai herbs including Thai basil, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and galangal, seasoned with fish sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and fresh chilli. The dish is characterised by its bold, fragrant aroma from the herb combination, a savoury-umami flavour profile from the fish and oyster sauces, and a tender yet slightly chewy beef texture. It is commonly served over steamed jasmine rice in Thai restaurants across Southeast Asia and Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g) and provides meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, and B vitamins from the beef. Sodium content is elevated due to the condiment-heavy sauce base, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index makes it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when rice portions are controlled. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Sanook Kitchen and is popular as a lunch or dinner main course.

เนื้อผัดสมุนไพรไทย nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried beef with Thai herbs was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy stir-fried beef with sauce data and FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous Thai dish data. GI was estimated from analogous stir-fried beef dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to estimation rather than direct measurement.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source database for this dish type. All other micronutrients with -1 values are not reported in the estimation sources used. Nutrient values represent a typical Thai restaurant preparation and may vary by restaurant, chef, and ingredient proportions. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu label '508D Stir-fried Beef with Thai Herb 泰国香料' to the canonical English form 'Stir-fried Beef with Thai Herbs'. The Chinese characters 泰国香料 (Thai spices/herbs) from the original menu label are preserved in the aliases. Sodium content is notably high due to fish sauce and oyster sauce; a 250 g serving provides approximately 1300 mg sodium. The dish is not confirmed halal as fish sauce and oyster sauce brands vary in halal certification status.
